key_insight: Personalizing a pneumatically actuated soft exoglove with hand scans and FEM improves anatomical fit and enables more targeted finger joint motion for rehabilitation.
engineering_relevance: The study shows how patient-specific geometry and pHRI modeling can guide soft actuator design toward better alignment, controllable MCP/PIP flexion, and more effective dexterous assistance.
method: Topological hand scans used to tailor glove geometry and mold fabrication to the user
Finite element analysis evaluated actuator bending and contact forces against a personalized finger model
Pneumatic pressure tests assessed static and dynamic flexion at MCP and PIP joints
evidence_signal: The abstract reports scan-based fabrication, FEM-based pHRI analysis, and pressure-control experiments demonstrating targeted joint mobilization and improved alignment with design changes.
watch_item: Results are based on a simplified personalized finger model, so full hand biomechanics, comfort, and long-term performance still need validation.
paper_title: Soft Robotic Exogloves for Dexterous Mobility -- Towards Personalized Rehabilitation
